content.full_text:
Re:
Fnrn Polrrrcer- PRACTTcEs CorraMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
August 21,2009
Phaedra A. Norton
City of Turlock
156 S. Broadway, Suite 240
Turlock, CA 95380-5454
Your Request for Informal Advice
Our file No.I-09-092
Dear Ms. Norton:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Turlock Mayor John
Lazar regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").' Because your request seeks general information and you have not provided any
information with respect to any specific govemmental decision, we are providing
informal assistance.z This letter is based on the facts presented; the Faii Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, please note that our advice is
based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the
application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest or Govemment Code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
Does Mayor Lazar have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prevent him
from participating in decisions involving the Turlock General Plan update?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
I Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 I l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
{p


File No. 09-092
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Mayor Lazar will have a disqualifying conflict of interest if the governmental
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on on" of his
identified economic interests.
FACTS
You represent Mayor John Lazar of Turlock. The Turlock City Council has hired
a consultant to update Turlock's general plan and to prepare an Environmental tmpact
Report ("EfR") for the updated general plan. MayorLazar will be participating in
decisions related to the general plan, including land use and policy decisions. -
Mayor Lazar i's a real estate agent in Turlock with Coldwell Banker Endsley &
Associates, where he has worked since 1993. Mayor Lazar primarily works in home
sales, though over the course ofhis career, he has also been * ag"ri for sales of
agricultural and commercial properties. During a telephone conversation with the Mayor,
he informed us that he works on 1007o commission, is an independent contractor with
Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates where he pays for his office space and expenses.
Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates is a franchise, managed by a broker-owner.
In the 12 months preceding these decisions, he will have earned at least $500 from the
brokerage. Mayor Lazar stated during our telephone conversation that he does not have
an equity interest in the brokerage for which he works. We do not know the identity of
any person from which he will have received at least $500 through his representation in a
real estate transaction in the 12 months prior to the decision at isiue. We do not have
information regarding what, if any, persons in connection with his work as a real estate
agent, have received a finder's fee (or other referral fee) for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who will have made a referral pursuant to a contract with the
broker.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(g).)


File No. 09-092
Page No. 3
Step One: Is Mayor Lazar a public official for purposes of the Act?
As Mayor of the City of Turlock, Mayor Lazar is a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise
use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of his economic interests. (Regulations 18702.1-
r8702.4.)
Step Two: Will MayorLazar be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision?
The city council will be reviewing and making decisions about the City's General
Plan. Consequently, Mayor Lazar may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use
his official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of his economic interests. (Regulations 18702.1-
t8702.4.)
Step Three: What are Mayor Lazar's economic interests?
A public official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
1. An economic interest in any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
2. An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)
3. An economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 18703(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "[ncome" includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual
or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a lO-percent interest or
greater. (Section 82030(a).)
4. An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)
5. An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his
or her immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)


File No. 09-092
Page No.4
Source of Income
Mayor Lazar is an independent contractor with Coldwell Banker Endsley &
Associates, where he earns commission income. Because his job as a real estate agent,
with clients in Turlock, will provide him with income (including promised incomej of
$500 or more within the 12 months prior to any govemmental diiision, sources of
income to Mayor Lazar as a result of his job constitute economic interests under the Act.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
Regulation 18703.3(c) (copy enclosed) contains disclosure and disqualification
requirements for any public official who receives commission income for services
rendered as a real estate agent. "Commission income" means gross payments received by
a public official as a result ofservices rendered as a broker, agent, or other salesperson
for a specific sale or similar transaction. (Reg. 18703.3(c)11).) commission income is
received when it is paid or credited. (Id.)
Under this regulation, a number of parties in a real estate transaction are
considered sources of income for a real estate agent. (.gee Regulation 1g703.3(cX2XC).)
That regulation provides that the following persons and entitiis are all sources of income,
in a specific sale or similar transaction, under the Act:
"(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the
agent works;
"(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and
"(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring
a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuantio
a contract with the broker." (Id.)
Mayor Lazar has an economic interest in the brokerage that he works under, as a
source of income, assuming he has received income from the brokerage that aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the time he makes the relevari decisions. other
persons referred to in Regulation 18703.3(cX2)(C) may also be sources of income to him,
of course, if he receives or is promised $500 or more by any of them for services
described in this regulation.
As previously stated, we do not have information regarding from which individual
persons Mayor Lazat will have received at least $500 through his iepresentation of them
in real estate transactions in the 12 months prior to the decis]ion. In addition, we do not
have information regarding which, if any, persons in connection with his work as a real
estate agent will have received a finder's fee (or other referral fee) for referring a party to
the transaction to the broker, or who will have made a referral pursuant to a co"ntract with
the broker that meets or exceeds the $500 / l2-month limit. Thlrefore, with regard to


File No. 09-092
Page No. 5
Mayor Lazat's real estate agent commission income, we will continue to examine only
the real estate brokerage as an identifiable economic interest under the Act.
Personal Finances.
Mayor Lazat has an economic interest in his personal finances and those of his
immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5; see Section BZ02g [defining
"immediate family" as one's spouse and dependent childrenl.) You have noi providid us
with facts regarding the personal finances of Mayor Lazar or his immediate fu*ily. W",
therefore, will not further analyze this factor.
Step 4: Are The Economic Interests Directly Or Indirectly Involved In The
Governmental Decision?
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decision in
question. (Regulations 18700(b)(4),19704(a).) Having establiihed the degree of
involvement, the official can then identify the materiaiity standard uppropiiut" to the
circumstances. (Regulation 18700(bx5;.j there are separate tests appfiea to real
property, sources of income, and personal finances, to determine whether an economic
interest is directly or indirectly involved in a specific governmental decision.
In analyzing whether a source of income is directly oiindirectly involved in a decision
before an official's agency, we look to Regulation 18704.1(a), which states in pertinent
part:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the sub]ect
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlemenito, or
contract with, the subject person."
Stated more specifically, a source of income is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that source Lf ir"o*., directly
or by an agen!, is a named party in, or is the subject of, a proceeding if tlhe
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocaiion of
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of
income. (Regulation 1870a. 1(a).)
Your facts do not suggest that Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates fall within
either of the tests set out in Regulation 18704.1(a). This source of income is therefore
indirectly involved in the decisions. (Regulation 1870a(a).) Likewise, you have not
provided any facts that suggest any other parties meet the criteria. Accordingly, our


File No. 09-092
Page No. 6
analysis proceeds under the assumption that none of the economic interests are directly
involved.
Step 5: Is The Financial Effect Of The Governmental Decision On The Economic
Interest Material?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulations
18700(a), 18705.) Different standards apply to determine whether a financial effect will
be material, depending upon the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the govemmental decision under consideration.
(Regulation 18705.2, (a) and (b).)
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a govemmental decision on an
indirectly involved source of income that is a business entity (as is the real estate
' brokerage for which the Mayor works as a real estate agenti is determined by applying
the materiality standards applicable to that particular business entity under neguiaiion
18705. 1(c). (Reg. 1870s.3(bxl).)
Whether the financial effect of a govemmental decision on an indirectly involved
business entity is mate_rial or not depends upon what type of business entity is involved.
(See Regulation 18705.1(cXl) - (4), copy enclosed.) Th"." are different tests depending
upon whether the business entity in question is listed in the Fortune 500, on the New
York stock Exchange, NAsDAe, or American Stock Exchange. (Id.) Generally, if a
business entity does not fit any of the preceding lists, it is sometimes referred to as a
"small business entity" and Regulation lg705.l(c)(a) applies.
Because you have not provided us with facts regarding what type of business
entity the real estate brokerage Mayor Lazar works forls. or facts reg"arding a specific
decision and the possible effect it might have on the real estate brokJrage fir which he
works, yoU must analyze - using the proper standards described above --whether the
financial effect of the described governmental decision would have a material effect on
the identified source of income. We enclose a copy of Regulation 18705.1 to assist you
in this analysis.
step 6: Is The Material Financiar Effect Reasonably Foreseeable?
lf a material financial effect on any economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, a
public official will be deemed to have a conflict of interest under the Act. (See
Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect on an economic interest is .teasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards
applicable to the particular economic interest under analysis will be met as a result of the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain,
however' or even substantially likely, to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must
be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. l9g; see Regulation


File No. 09-092
Page No. 7
18706(b) [listing five factors to consider in determining whether the material financial
effect of a govemmental decision will be reasonably foieseeablel.)
Regulations 18706(b) and (c) set out a list of non-exclusive factors to consider in
determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable and serve as a general
guideline to use in determining whether this standard is met. Those subsectionJ state:
"(b) In determining whether a govemmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an economic interest as
defined in subdivision (a) above, the following factors should be
considered. These factors are not intended to be an exclusive list of the
relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines:
"(l) The extent to which the official or the official's iource o1income has
engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business activity in the
jurisdiction;
"(2)The market share held by the official or the official,s source of
income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official's source of income has
competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the gor"*.rrtal decision in question; and
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the rnaterial financial effect is
contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental
decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the official's ug"n"y.
"(c) Possession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any other
professional license, without regard to the official's business u"iirity o,
likely business activity, does not in itself make a material financial Lff""t
on the official's economic interest reasonably foieseeable.,,
The factors presented above, and any other potentially relevant factors, must be
examined against each economic interest identified. Because such an analysis involves a
fact-sensitive inquiry, and we do not have enough facts regarding the relevant inquiry
(e.g., what type of business entity the real estate brokerage fo. *-hi.h the Mayor works is,
under Regulation 18705.1(c)), Mayor Lazar must determine how these factois apply to
his situation.
Step Seven: The "Public Generally, Exception
A public official will not be disqualified from participating in a governmental
decision if the proposed decision affects the public official's economic interests in a
manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the
"public generally." (Sec^tion 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) Under the two prong test, to
meet the requirements of this exception, the decision must financially affeci a .,s-ignificant
segment" of the public generally in "substantially the same manner" that it would affect


File No. 09-092
Page No. 8
the public official. (Regulation 18707.1.) Different tests apply depending upon the
economic interest being examined.
Again, because we do not have sufficient facts to apply this exception, we are
unable to provide further analysis.
Step Eight: The "Legally Required Participation,, Exception
You have not provided any facts suggesting that this exception would apply.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at tgrol 322-566d.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
t*At^ tt
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
Enclosure

or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that

individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which

As Mayor of the City of Turlock, Mayor Lazar is a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise
use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of his economic interests. (Regulations 18702.1-
r8702.4.)
Step Two: Will MayorLazar be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision?
The city council will be reviewing and making decisions about the City's General
Plan. Consequently, Mayor Lazar may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use
his official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of his economic interests. (Regulations 18702.1-
t8702.4.)
Step Three: What are Mayor Lazar's economic interests?
A public official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
1. An economic interest in any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
2. An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)
3. An economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 18703(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "[ncome" includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual
or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a lO-percent interest or
greater. (Section 82030(a).)
4. An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)
5. An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his
or her immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Mayor Lazar is an independent contractor with Coldwell Banker Endsley &
Associates, where he earns commission income. Because his job as a real estate agent,
with clients in Turlock, will provide him with income (including promised incomej of
$500 or more within the 12 months prior to any govemmental diiision, sources of

(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
Regulation 18703.3(c) (copy enclosed) contains disclosure and disqualification

rendered as a real estate agent. "Commission income" means gross payments received by
a public official as a result ofservices rendered as a broker, agent, or other salesperson
for a specific sale or similar transaction. (Reg. 18703.3(c)11).) commission income is
received when it is paid or credited. (Id.)
Under this regulation, a number of parties in a real estate transaction are
considered sources of income for a real estate agent. (.gee Regulation 1g703.3(cX2XC).)
That regulation provides that the following persons and entitiis are all sources of income,
in a specific sale or similar transaction, under the Act:
"(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the
agent works;
"(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and
"(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring
a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuantio
a contract with the broker." (Id.)
Mayor Lazar has an economic interest in the brokerage that he works under, as a
source of income, assuming he has received income from the brokerage that aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the time he makes the relevari decisions. other
persons referred to in Regulation 18703.3(cX2)(C) may also be sources of income to him,
of course, if he receives or is promised $500 or more by any of them for services
described in this regulation.
As previously stated, we do not have information regarding from which individual
persons Mayor Lazat will have received at least $500 through his iepresentation of them
in real estate transactions in the 12 months prior to the decis]ion. In addition, we do not
have information regarding which, if any, persons in connection with his work as a real
estate agent will have received a finder's fee (or other referral fee) for referring a party to
the transaction to the broker, or who will have made a referral pursuant to a co"ntract with
the broker that meets or exceeds the $500 / l2-month limit. Thlrefore, with regard to
Mayor Lazat's real estate agent commission income, we will continue to examine only
the real estate brokerage as an identifiable economic interest under the Act.
Personal Finances.
Mayor Lazat has an economic interest in his personal finances and those of his
immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5; see Section BZ02g [defining
"immediate family" as one's spouse and dependent childrenl.) You have noi providid us
with facts regarding the personal finances of Mayor Lazar or his immediate fu*ily. W",
therefore, will not further analyze this factor.
Step 4: Are The Economic Interests Directly Or Indirectly Involved In The
Governmental Decision?
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decision in
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: . (Regulations 18700(b)(4),19704(a).) Having establiihed the degree of
involvement, the official can then identify the materiaiity standard uppropiiut" to the
circumstances. (Regulation 18700(bx5;.j there are separate tests appfiea to real
property, sources of income, and personal finances, to determine whether an economic
interest is directly or indirectly involved in a specific governmental decision.
In analyzing whether a source of income is directly oiindirectly involved in a decision
before an official's agency, we look to Regulation 18704.1(a), which states in pertinent
part:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the sub]ect
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlemenito, or
contract with, the subject person."
Stated more specifically, a source of income is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that source Lf ir"o*., directly
or by an agen!, is a named party in, or is the subject of, a proceeding if tlhe
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocaiion of
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of
income. (Regulation 1870a. 1(a).)
Your facts do not suggest that Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates fall within
either of the tests set out in Regulation 18704.1(a). This source of income is therefore
indirectly involved in the decisions. (Regulation 1870a(a).) Likewise, you have not
provided any facts that suggest any other parties meet the criteria. Accordingly, our
analysis proceeds under the assumption that none of the economic interests are directly
involved.
Step 5: Is The Financial Effect Of The Governmental Decision On The Economic
Interest Material?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulations
18700(a), 18705.) Different standards apply to determine whether a financial effect will
be material, depending upon the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the govemmental decision under consideration.
(Regulation 18705.2, (a) and (b).)
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a govemmental decision on an
indirectly involved source of income that is a business entity (as is the real estate
' brokerage for which the Mayor works as a real estate agenti is determined by applying
the materiality standards applicable to that particular business entity under neguiaiion
18705. 1(c). (Reg. 1870s.3(bxl).)
Whether the financial effect of a govemmental decision on an indirectly involved
business entity is mate_rial or not depends upon what type of business entity is involved.
(See Regulation 18705.1(cXl) - (4), copy enclosed.) Th"." are different tests depending
upon whether the business entity in question is listed in the Fortune 500, on the New
York stock Exchange, NAsDAe, or American Stock Exchange. (Id.) Generally, if a
business entity does not fit any of the preceding lists, it is sometimes referred to as a
"small business entity" and Regulation lg705.l(c)(a) applies.
Because you have not provided us with facts regarding what type of business
entity the real estate brokerage Mayor Lazar works forls. or facts reg"arding a specific
decision and the possible effect it might have on the real estate brokJrage fir which he
works, yoU must analyze - using the proper standards described above --whether the
financial effect of the described governmental decision would have a material effect on
the identified source of income. We enclose a copy of Regulation 18705.1 to assist you
in this analysis.
step 6: Is The Material Financiar Effect Reasonably Foreseeable?
lf a material financial effect on any economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, a
public official will be deemed to have a conflict of interest under the Act. (See
Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect on an economic interest is .teasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards
applicable to the particular economic interest under analysis will be met as a result of the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain,
however' or even substantially likely, to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must
be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. l9g; see Regulation
18706(b) [listing five factors to consider in determining whether the material financial
effect of a govemmental decision will be reasonably foieseeablel.)
Regulations 18706(b) and (c) set out a list of non-exclusive factors to consider in
determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable and serve as a general
guideline to use in determining whether this standard is met. Those subsectionJ state:
"(b) In determining whether a govemmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an economic interest as
defined in subdivision (a) above, the following factors should be
considered. These factors are not intended to be an exclusive list of the
relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines:
"(l) The extent to which the official or the official's iource o1income has
engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business activity in the
jurisdiction;
"(2)The market share held by the official or the official,s source of
income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official's source of income has
competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the gor"*.rrtal decision in question; and
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the rnaterial financial effect is
contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental
decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the official's ug"n"y.
"(c) Possession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any other
professional license, without regard to the official's business u"iirity o,
likely business activity, does not in itself make a material financial Lff""t
on the official's economic interest reasonably foieseeable.,,
The factors presented above, and any other potentially relevant factors, must be
examined against each economic interest identified. Because such an analysis involves a
fact-sensitive inquiry, and we do not have enough facts regarding the relevant inquiry
(e.g., what type of business entity the real estate brokerage fo. *-hi.h the Mayor works is,
under Regulation 18705.1(c)), Mayor Lazar must determine how these factois apply to
his situation.
Step Seven: The "Public Generally, Exception
A public official will not be disqualified from participating in a governmental
decision if the proposed decision affects the public official's economic interests in a
manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the
"public generally." (Sec^tion 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) Under the two prong test, to
meet the requirements of this exception, the decision must financially affeci a .,s-ignificant
segment" of the public generally in "substantially the same manner" that it would affect
the public official. (Regulation 18707.1.) Different tests apply depending upon the
economic interest being examined.
Again, because we do not have sufficient facts to apply this exception, we are
unable to provide further analysis.
Step Eight: The "Legally Required Participation,, Exception
You have not provided any facts suggesting that this exception would apply.

CONCLUSION: Mayor Lazar will have a disqualifying conflict of interest if the governmental
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on on" of his
identified economic interests.

Norton City of Turlock 156 S. Broadway, Suite 240 Turlock, CA 95380-5454 Your Request for Informal Advice Our file No.I-09-092 Dear Ms. Norton: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Turlock Mayor John Lazar regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Because your request seeks general information and you have not provided any information with respect to any specific govemmental decision, we are providing informal assistance.z This letter is based on the facts presented; the Faii Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. QUESTTON Does Mayor Lazar have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prevent him from participating in decisions involving the Turlock General Plan update? I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. I Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83 I l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) {p File No. 09-092 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Mayor Lazar will have a disqualifying conflict of interest if the governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on on" of his identified economic interests. FACTS You represent Mayor John Lazar of Turlock. The Turlock City Council has hired a consultant to update Turlock's general plan and to prepare an Environmental tmpact Report ("EfR") for the updated general plan. MayorLazar will be participating in decisions related to the general plan, including land use and policy decisions. - Mayor Lazar i's a real estate agent in Turlock with Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates, where he has worked since 1993. Mayor Lazar primarily works in home sales, though over the course ofhis career, he has also been * ag"ri for sales of agricultural and commercial properties. During a telephone conversation with the Mayor, he informed us that he works on 1007o commission, is an independent contractor with Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates where he pays for his office space and expenses. Coldwell Banker Endsley & Associates is a franchise, managed by a broker-owner. In the 12 months preceding these decisions, he will have earned at least $500 from the brokerage. Mayor Lazar stated during our
